title: Videoconferencing Booms on SARS Fears
author: Ry Crozier
abstract: Electronic News (Electronics News Australia) reports videoconferencing surge tied to SARS-driven travel bans. Aberdeen Group analysts Russ Craig and Peter Kastner warn that the entire electronics assembly industry has 'critical, no-second-source dependence' on China-made components — a vulnerability SARS could expose. They frame the supply-chain risk as solvable only via aggressive public-health response.
importance_rationale: Early articulation of China-electronics-supply-chain concentration risk — the same framework deployed in COVID-19, US-China decoupling, and CHIPS Act discussions two decades later.
relevance_rationale: Component-supply-chain concentration concerns remain front-and-center in 2024-2026 geopolitics; the Craig/Kastner framing reads as freshly relevant.
prescience_rationale: The 'no-second-source dependence on China' diagnosis was vindicated repeatedly: COVID-19 (2020), Taiwan-Strait crisis discussions, semiconductor sovereignty policy. Ahead of its time in 2003.
